title: The Toucher
content:
آ آ آ آ He was a jobbing hand from the printersï؟½ flat. His name was Raymond Cato, but he acquired ï؟½Toucherï؟½ as a complimentary title when we knew him better. He was tall, sallow, languid and distressingly impecunious. I put it that way because Mr Catoï؟½s impecuniosity was more a trait of character than the result of misfortune. He was the sort of young man who would have been impecunious had he been born to ten thousand a year. He was slovenly in his dress, and his trousers were always worn to strings at the heels, and this fringe collected various foreign bodies, which dragged after him as be walked, Raymond being too languid or too indifferent to shake them off. You got to know when Toucher was coming by the clatter of vagrant articles attached to his trousers fringe. He once towed a disused fish-tin after him through a whole hot afternoon. That will give you an idea of the sort of person Raymond Cato was. But this depraved young man, while apparently sleeping against a case, could paw type with miraculous speed and precision, and he handled the most intricate jobs with absolute certainty when under the influence of two buckets of very bad beer.

آ آ آ آ Mr Cato had only been ten days in the factory when be came to the packerï؟½s board and leaned there. There were two peach-nuts, a metal rule, and the rind off a tin of red ink dangling at his fetlock. He passed his hand wearily over his brow, brushing back his long, black hair, and rested his eyes on the packer. Raymondï؟½s eyes were large and dark, and suffused with an overwhelming sadness. The Toucher owed his success largely to those appealing eyes.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Sï؟½pose we do a break, Mills.ï؟½ he said, joylessly.

آ آ آ آ Feathers looked at him with bitter reproach. The remark was an invitation to execute a strategic exit by the lift door, and drink pints, and Feathers was up to his ears in work.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½Ow thï؟½ell can Iï؟½ he said, pointing at the long list of orders, ï؟½ï؟½ntï؟½ the whole flaminï؟½ warehouse whoopinï؟½ fer goods?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, well,ï؟½ said Cam, resignedly. ï؟½I had a tizzie, my last and itï؟½s so lonely I reckoned Iï؟½d let it go.ï؟½ He took the coin out, turned it over in his fingers, and sighed. ï؟½Left blooming alone,ï؟½ he said.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Down to it, are yeh?ï؟½ asked the packer with sympathy. ï؟½Thereï؟½ve been times when Iï؟½ve ï؟½ad tï؟½ run by ther pub with me eyes shut meself, ï؟½nï؟½ I know what it is.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Fair on my knuckle-bone,ï؟½ said the printer. ï؟½But a man doesnï؟½t care on his own account. Itï؟½s the old lady.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Feathers looked sidelong. Feathersï؟½s class is always suspicious of sentiment; but there was no snivelling in Raymondï؟½s tone. His expression was that of a strong man who bears his troubles bravely, and his accent hinted at a profound emotion kept well in hand.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tribulation in thor ï؟½appy ï؟½ome?ï؟½ queried Feathers, warily.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Slathers, Mills, old man.ï؟½ Raymond Cato turned his shoulder, but the strong composed voice continued presently: ï؟½The mother, you know. Seen better days, George; good family. Dreadfully ill, andï؟½, here the voice was almost cold, ï؟½and I havenï؟½t the half-crown to pay for her medicine tonight.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Jimmy Jee!ï؟½ murmured Feathers. He fingered a solitary coin in his pocket lovingly, drew it out, and laid it on the bench.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½Ereï؟½s arf er dollar you can have ther use of,ï؟½ he said.

آ آ آ آ There was just the faintest suggestion of a start, the most momentary hint of eagerness, in Raymondï؟½s descent upon the money. The thing had been easier than he expected. Mills noticed the start, and a pang of repentance shot through him. Cato realized his mistake instantly. He placed a firm retainer on the half-crown, and slid it back towards the packer.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½No, no, old man,ï؟½ he said, ï؟½you canï؟½t spare this.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Garn!ï؟½ retorted the packer, with simulated indifference, ï؟½get a ï؟½ammerlock on it.ï؟½ With a flash of diplomacy, he added; ï؟½Itï؟½s onï؟½y till Saterdee, anyhow.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, all right, Geordie, till Saturday. Youï؟½re a good sort.ï؟½ Catoï؟½s tone implied that the time might soon come when he would show his gratitude by dying for the packer. He took up the half-crown slowly, reluctantly, and went gently downstairs.

آ آ آ آ For a minute Feathers gazed fixedly at the blank wall before him, forgetting his work.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Iï؟½ve been stabbed,ï؟½ he whispered. ï؟½Thatï؟½s it, er clean stab. ï؟½Nï؟½ I was beginninï؟½ tï؟½ think I was grown up. Geordie Mills, you ainï؟½t fit tï؟½ be allowed out without yer aunt.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Feathers was not a man to show his wounds. He said nothing, and in the course of a day or so the town traveller came to him with his trouble.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½What of young Cato, the comp with the fatal beauty of a consumptive nun,ï؟½ said Goudy; ï؟½is he a confidence trick or what?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Heï؟½s ther pride iv ther famï؟½ly, ï؟½nï؟½ ther sole support iv all his bed-ridden relations,ï؟½ replied the packer.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Well, heï؟½s touched me three times in a week, and Iï؟½m as Scotch as most people.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Howï؟½d he plead? Was it corf drops for his sick sister, ï؟½r funï؟½ral expenses fer his dead brother-in-law left over from last week consequence iv thor ï؟½ard ï؟½earted undertaker ref usinï؟½ delivery at ther graveside?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½I donï؟½t know how he did it,ï؟½ mused Goudy, scratching his whiskers. ï؟½He must have used laughing gas. ï؟½Twas absolutely painless extraction.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½Eï؟½s er hartist, got er touch like velvet. ï؟½E putï؟½s ther acid on soï؟½t yeh think itï؟½s ther milk iv ï؟½uman kindness.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hello,ï؟½ cried Goudy, ï؟½heï؟½s dipped up something of yours then, has he?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Feathers almost blushed. ï؟½Come off,ï؟½ he said, with a shade less than his usual confidence. ï؟½Ther gay deceivers donï؟½t twitter tï؟½ me. Iï؟½ve bin too long out iv thï؟½ egg.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, yes, yes, yes, yes. I was forgetting,ï؟½ chortled the town traveller, softly. ï؟½You are Abdul the Wise and Wonderful. You are the great Take-Down. The bad men go home by the back streets to escape your machinations, and youï؟½ve brought hundreds of spielers to destitution.ï؟½ And Goudy went about his business, whistling absently the offensive refrain of ï؟½Muggins the Jugginsï؟½.

آ آ آ آ Feathers pasted a label, and attached it to a parcel with unwonted precision. He hated Goudy when he used big words. ï؟½Anyï؟½ow,ï؟½ said Mills, ï؟½Iï؟½m not er soft thing for beginners. Iï؟½m not sich er snag fer sharpers that ther boys practise on me.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ This little disputation rendered it unnecessary for Feathers to breathe a word about the manner in which the Toucher had stabbed him. The town traveller, too, concluded that his reputation as a business man would not be enhanced by further discussion, and so Raymond Cato was free to play upon the tender buds of human sympathy in the lean and dusty breast of Samuel Ellis, foreman of the flat.

آ آ آ آ Ellis was cutting a ream of tinfoil for which Toucher was waiting, and Toucherï؟½s sadness was dramatic. He sighed heavily, and a tear fell on the polished platform of the guillotine. Raymond gently wiped it off with a piece of waste.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Arenï؟½t you well, then, Mr Cato?ï؟½ said Ellis. The woeful foreman even ministered Billy, the personal devil from the printing flat.

آ آ آ آ Raymond Cato started like a guilty thing, moved away a few steps, changed his mind, as if convinced that Fuzzy was the kind of man in whom any stricken soul might confide, returned, and placed the photograph of a baby boy on the platform. The baby was taken on the half-shell, and looked like one of those remarkably fine children who earn an excellent living by posing as examples of the results of feeding on Somebody s infantsï؟½ Condition Mixture. The young man had found the photograph in a drawer downstairs that afternoon.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½You are a father yourself?ï؟½ he asked.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Noï؟½ said Ellis, ï؟½but I have a sister what is. Five she has.ï؟½ And then, with a lugubrious effort to show interest, ï؟½Whatï؟½s he, a boy or a girl?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½You wouldnï؟½t know him now,ï؟½ Raymond said, huskily. ï؟½Skin and bone, skin and bone. And he cries to me so pitifully; and what can I do?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Have you tried castor oil?ï؟½ asked Ellis, vaguely.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½My God!ï؟½ said the young man, in a low, terrible voice ï؟½if that boy dies, I donï؟½t care what becomes of me.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Go on!ï؟½ murmured the foreman, deeply touched.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½And whatï؟½s a man to do?, what can a man do? They order expensive things, chicken in champagne. I tell you, I feel sometimes that I could commit murder to procure the money that might be the saving of my boy.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½I wouldnï؟½t if I was you,ï؟½ Ellis said. He was quite agitated. He looked at Cato with pitiful eyes. ï؟½I really wouldnï؟½t you know.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Raymond snatched up the tinfoil and walked away, stopped, and came back, looked Ellis square in the eye, steadily, for nearly half a minute, and then he said deliberately, emphatically, as if putting the foreman to the test of his life.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Would you lend me half-a-sovereign?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Yes, yes; of course,ï؟½ stammered Fuzzy. He went to his vest pocket for the money. His hands quite trembled with eagerness as he handed it to Cato.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½But mind, now, no murders,ï؟½ he said.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Donï؟½t speak to me or I shall break down,ï؟½ faltered Raymond Cato, in the character of one unaccustomed to such great kindness and he fled from the flat.

آ آ آ آ Bennoï؟½s turn came later. Be also went to Feathers with the tale. He tried to speak as one with unshaken confidence in the Toucher, but there were subtle doubts hovering at the back of his head.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Got ther luck iv er lame cat, Cato has,ï؟½ he said, fiddling with the packerï؟½s scales. ï؟½Dï؟½jer hear about him?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Feathers fanned out a ream, and knocked it up like a second Cinquevalli; then he sought a hiding place for the incriminating tobacco juice, and spat with judgement.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Benno,ï؟½ he Said, with aggravating conviction, ï؟½yet cominï؟½ tï؟½ me with ther story iv yet shame?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Giveï؟½s er charnce,ï؟½ retorted the clerk.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Ther lad below been nibblinï؟½ yet ear, ï؟½n thinkinï؟½ iv doinï؟½ er mag erbout sheddinï؟½ yer beans in ther sacred cause iv charity, but it donï؟½t go.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Youï؟½re one what knows,ï؟½ sneered Benno.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Well, er blokeï؟½s lived er bit,ï؟½ admitted the oracle. ï؟½Chat aloud, Benjamin, Iï؟½m waitinï؟½ fer ther ï؟½arrowinï؟½ details. Was it his pore ole father, what was er Dook once, crying all night with dried peas in his appendicitis, him beinï؟½ er victim to ï؟½em ï؟½r his lady mother whoï؟½s liï؟½ble tï؟½ become sober at any moment if ther charitably inclined donï؟½t come to ï؟½er assistance in ï؟½er hour iv need? ï؟½Cause I may tell yeh, Benno ther seraph, that Catoï؟½s parents ï؟½ye both bin missinï؟½ for years. They saw what he was cominï؟½ to when he was five, ï؟½nï؟½ did er guy, leavinï؟½ him tï؟½ ther mercy iv er crool world on er pub doorstep.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Nothinï؟½ like it,ï؟½ said the clerek. ï؟½He had a naxident, swallered er thick-un. I was workinï؟½ a bit late day before yesï؟½day, ï؟½nï؟½ ï؟½long erbout art past six who should come sprintinï؟½ upstairs but me nibs, paleï؟½s er blessed egg, hair on end, fair dilly. The bums was in his house fer rent, ï؟½nï؟½ he was hurryinï؟½ ï؟½ome with ther quid heï؟½d got advanced stuck in his tooth box, when he butted into some gazob in ther street, ï؟½nï؟½ down went ther thick-un. He was tearinï؟½ ratty tï؟½ raise another jim. Er bloke he knows promised him four half dollars, ï؟½nï؟½ he come tï؟½ me fer ther rest, seeinï؟½ I was a cobber in er way, ï؟½nï؟½ his ï؟½ome sweet ï؟½ome was goinï؟½ tï؟½ pieces on his ï؟½ands. Acourse, I parted me arf jim, couldnï؟½t have ther brick face tï؟½ do less under ther circs. ï؟½Twas onï؟½y fer er day, he said, cause he was goinï؟½ under er operation yesï؟½day ter recover then lost goblin.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½Nï؟½ was ther operation er triumph iv surgical skill?ï؟½ asked Feathers. ï؟½I know, they recovered ten sovs ï؟½nï؟½ er gold watch, ï؟½nï؟½ Catoï؟½s payinï؟½ two ï؟½undred per cent dividends tï؟½ ther share holders. Yer goinï؟½ tï؟½ hinvite me out tï؟½ ther parlour bar, ï؟½nï؟½ plaster me in ï؟½nï؟½ out with sixpenny drinks.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½You ainï؟½t bettinï؟½ on that.ï؟½ Benno was gloomy now. ï؟½No; his nibs come tï؟½ me yesï؟½day ï؟½nï؟½ said heï؟½d seen his medical adviser, ï؟½if the blessed operation ï؟½ud cost one ï؟½nï؟½ a ï؟½arf, ï؟½nï؟½ that heï؟½d go on with it if Iï؟½d contribit another dollar.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Nï؟½ yeh did it, yeh did it!ï؟½ yelled Feathers in ecstasy. ï؟½Yeh sank another five shillinï؟½ in yer wild cat. Oh, hereï؟½s Bertie off ther boat, hereï؟½s Little Willie. Some kind gentleman hold his purse while he gets his ï؟½ead read. Yeh did it!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½Tainï؟½t possible,ï؟½ retorted Benno.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, ainï؟½t it? Well, yï؟½ orter wear ear muffs this bitinï؟½ weather, silly boy.ï؟½ Feathers slapped at Benno with feminine affectation. ï؟½Why, Cato never had no ï؟½ome that it ï؟½ud pay to put er bailiff in at tuppence er day. Heï؟½s got er leaky room at mother Spargoï؟½s, in Williamson Street, fer which ï؟½e owes regular every Fridee, ï؟½nï؟½ he dosses on er two-feather bed, weather permittinï؟½.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Evidently it was Mr Catoï؟½s intention to try the acid on Feathers again. He approached the packer a few days after Bennoï؟½s confession, and he had the air of one patient under great provocation. The packerï؟½s hand went to his ear instinctively.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½How would you take a good thing for the Handicap tomorrow. Feathers?ï؟½ said the comp. ï؟½Youï؟½re the straightest man here, and the only one Iï؟½d let into this with me.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ The packer did not reply for a few moments. He lingered for effect, chewing absently.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½This ther one yeh worked on Billy?ï؟½ He spoke without interest.

آ آ آ آ Raymond looked at Mills with the reproachful eyes of a starved dog.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½You donï؟½t trust me, George,ï؟½ he said.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Chattinï؟½ iv good things,ï؟½ continued Feathers, ï؟½whatï؟½s gone wrong with that beautiful arf-dollar iv mine?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Did you lend me half-a-dollar? Of course, of course, you did, Oh, thatï؟½s all right, old fellow.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Thatï؟½s er weight off me mind,ï؟½ sighed Feathers. ï؟½Iï؟½ve bin expectinï؟½ that two ï؟½nï؟½ er tanner home. Iï؟½ve bin sittinï؟½ up iv nights waitinï؟½ fer it cominï؟½, ï؟½nï؟½ I got tï؟½ thinkinï؟½ yeh was neglectinï؟½ me. Ray, ï؟½nï؟½ devotinï؟½ all yer money tï؟½ good works.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ The compï؟½s sadness was intensified by Millsï؟½ sarcasm. ï؟½Surely a miserable half dollar wouldnï؟½t hurt you,ï؟½ he said.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½It ï؟½urts me pride,ï؟½ answered Feathers. ï؟½It ï؟½urts me tï؟½ think Iï؟½ve bin done on ther grid. The hagonyï؟½s somethinï؟½ awful, ï؟½nï؟½ Iï؟½ve gotter get that ï؟½arf-bull ï؟½r sometinï؟½ dangerous may set in.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, very well, you shall have it immediately.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Donï؟½t get runninï؟½ me down with it, Ray, giï؟½ me time tï؟½ break ther glad tidinï؟½s tï؟½ me famï؟½ly.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Raymond Cato spent the dinner-hour discussing the drama and kindred arts with Martha Pilcher, and in the afternoon he sent the half-crown to Mills in an envelope inscribed, ï؟½With R.C.ï؟½s compliments.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ By this time the Toucher had almost exhausted the male employees. There were many aching victims downstairs, and his title was recognized all through the factory. He paid a pressing creditor when he could borrow the necessary sum from another subject, but that did not happen often. Strangers were continually asking for him at the door in the lane, and people would await his coming for an hour after he had gone home over the adjoining roofs, and down via an accommodating tea warehouse with an open-air staircase. Raymond borrowed only small sums from the Beauties, but he was very successful with them. The other young men always approached the girls in a mood of artful and distressing levity, but the Toucher was very grave with them, and always sympathetic whatever the complaint might be, and there was scarcely a Beauty who hadnï؟½t a complaint of some kind by her, if only as a convenient theme of conversation. Half-a-dozen of them would have died for the bibulous comp despite the bottle-ohï؟½s stock garnered in the trouser fringe at his boot heels, and every paster but one seemed good for a shilling in exchange for a sigh or two and a pathetic glance from his soulful eyes. Those eyes were irresistible when Raymond was really thirsty.

آ آ آ آ The one girl whom Raymond had not been seen to approach for a little loan was Miss Eva Magill. Miss Eva was not as young as some, and she had no ï؟½cobbersï؟½ amongst the Beauties. She was demure and steadfast, came softly into the factory with down cast eyes, worked quietly and persistently in an out-of-the-way corner, with drooping lids, and passed out again, noiselessly, with eyes unlifted, and was wholly unknown. She was disliked and respected by the whole factory.

آ آ آ آ Toucherï؟½s finish was sudden and dramatic. One afternoon faint echoes of a warm debate came up from the warehouse. Presently Miss Magill was sent for. She went down, with her apron over her face, weeping. She returned in a quarter of an hour, still weeping, went to the dressing-room corner, and had a fit of hysterics, concluding with a dead faint.

آ آ آ آ Billy the Boy bobbed up a few moments later. ï؟½Er fair ole beano!ï؟½ he gasped. ï؟½They ye dumped Toucher in ther dust-box for immedjit removal. Spats wanted tï؟½ send fer the Johns, Heï؟½s done Magill in fer ï؟½er little bit.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ A voice of authority roared below, and Billy ducked down again, but Feathers had the whole story before evening.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½E bin givinï؟½ Magill guff erbout marriage,ï؟½ Mills explained to the town traveller, ï؟½ï؟½nï؟½ ther spiel was good fer fifteen jim ther savinï؟½s iv er lifetime, which ther dear boy was goinï؟½ t risk in ther comfits of a ï؟½ome, but which ï؟½eï؟½s bounced down et ther two-up.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½That pale maiden lady with the little lisp?ï؟½ exclaimed Goudy, in amazement. ï؟½Why, the very thoughts of man gave her all the unfavourable symptoms mentioned in a quack advertisement.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Gï؟½out, that sortï؟½s sweet ï؟½nï؟½ easy tï؟½ ther lad that ainï؟½t punched off with a frown,ï؟½ retorted Feathers. ï؟½Her brother took er tumble, ï؟½nï؟½ heï؟½s bin here, wantinï؟½ tï؟½ hit Cato clean out, ï؟½nï؟½ put him away over ther ï؟½olidays for false pretensions, ï؟½r larceny ez er bailiff, ï؟½r somethinï؟½, only thï؟½ girl wouldnï؟½t hear iv it. Sheï؟½s bin squealinï؟½ rounï؟½ all ther afternoon that Raymondï؟½s true to ï؟½er, true ez true, she donï؟½t care.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½The infernal scoundrel,ï؟½ snorted Goudy. ï؟½He ought to be hanged!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½Eï؟½s er naughty tease,ï؟½ Mills soliloquized, ï؟½ï؟½nï؟½ ï؟½e did Scotty fer er tray-bit, ï؟½anginï؟½s too good fer him!ï؟½
meaning:
About: Edward George Dyson (1865-1931) was an Australian poet, journalist and short story writer.

He was born at Morrisons near Ballarat in March 1865. His father, George Dyson, arrived in Australia in 1852 and after working on various diggings became a mining engineer, his mother came from a life of refinement in England. The family led a roving life during Dyson's childhood, moving successively to Alfredton, Bendigo, Ballarat and Alfredton again. Unconsciously the boy was storing for future use the life of the miners, farmers and bushmen, among whom he lived. At 12 he began to work as an assistant to a travelling draper, after that was a whimboy in a mine, and for two or three years an assistant in a factory at Melbourne. This was followed by work in a newspaper office. At 19 he began writing verse, and a few years later embarked on a life of free-lance journalism which lasted until his death. His first notable work was "The Golden Shanty", which appeared in the Bulletin, and many other short stories followed. In 1896 he published a volume of poems, Rhymes from the Mines, and in 1898 the first collection of his short stories, Below and On Top. In 1901 his first long story The Gold-stealers was published in London, which was followed by In the Roaring Fifties in 1906. In the same year appeared Fact'ry 'Ands, a series of more or less connected sketches dealing with factory life in Melbourne in a vein of humour. Various other stories and collections of stories were published in the Bookstall Series and will be found listed in Miller's Australian Literature. Another volume of verse Hello, Soldier! appeared in 1919. All through the years Dyson did an enormous amount of work until he broke down under the strain and died after a long illness on 22 August 1931. He married Miss Jackson who survived him with one daughter.

Edward Dyson was the brother of Will Dyson and Ambrose Dyson.
